content_en:
The middle volumes of Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh cover the Umayyad caliphate (41–132 AH / 661–750 CE) and the Abbasid caliphate (132–622 AH / 750–1225 CE), representing the longest continuous section of the chronicle. Ibn al-Athir's treatment of these dynasties reflects both his admiration for Islamic political achievement and his willingness to record failures, excesses, and internal conflicts without idealization.

The Umayyad Period

Ibn al-Athir opens the Umayyad period with the caliphate of Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan, treating the civil wars that brought the Umayyads to power with characteristic balance. He records the events of Siffin, the arbitration at Adhruh, and the establishment of Umayyad rule in Damascus without taking sides in the theological and political disputes that divided subsequent Muslim opinion. His priority is to reconstruct what happened, not to adjudicate who was right.

The conquests under the Umayyads receive detailed coverage. The expansion into North Africa and Andalus under al-Walid ibn Abd al-Malik, the campaigns into Central Asia under Qutayba ibn Muslim, and the naval expeditions into the Mediterranean are narrated with the enthusiasm of a historian who appreciates military logistics and geography. Ibn al-Athir notes the territorial extent of the Umayyad state at its peak as the largest empire the world had yet seen.

He also covers the internal tensions: the Kharijite movements, the revolts of Ibn al-Zubayr, the tragedy of Karbala, and the theological controversies over free will and predestination that animated early Islamic intellectual life. These sections demonstrate that Al-Kamil is not merely a political chronicle but an attempt to understand the forces that shape Muslim civilization.

The Abbasid Golden Age

The Abbasid period is presented in two phases: the era of power (750–950 CE) and the era of fragmentation (950–1258 CE). The early Abbasid centuries under al-Mansur, Harun al-Rashid, and al-Mamun represent for Ibn al-Athir the apex of Islamic civilization: the translation movement that absorbed Greek philosophy and science, the flourishing of Baghdad as the world's greatest city, the codification of Islamic law, and the development of hadith scholarship.

The accounts of Harun al-Rashid are among the most vivid in the chronicle, drawing on court traditions about his legendary generosity, his judicial interest, and his personal piety alongside his political pragmatism. Ibn al-Athir records the Barmakid episode — the sudden fall of the powerful Persian vizier family — as an example of the unpredictable nature of royal favor.

Fragmentation and the Coming of the Mongols

The later Abbasid period in Ibn al-Athir's account is a chronicle of fragmentation: the rise of autonomous dynasties (Buyids, Samanids, Hamdanids, Fatimids), the Crusader invasions of the Levant, and the emergence of Seljuk Turkish power. Ibn al-Athir lived through the beginning of the Mongol invasions and records their early campaigns with horror. His eyewitness accounts and reports from refugees make this one of the most powerful testimonies to the catastrophe that the Mongols brought upon Islamic civilization.
content_id:
Volume-volume tengah dari Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh mencakup kekhalifahan Umayyah (41–132 H / 661–750 M) dan kekhalifahan Abbasiyah (132–622 H / 750–1225 M), yang merupakan bagian kronik yang paling panjang. Perlakuan Ibn al-Athir terhadap kedua dinasti ini mencerminkan baik kekagumannya terhadap kekuatan dan perluasan wilayah Islam, maupun rasa prihatinnya terhadap kemunduran moral dan perpecahan politik yang menggerogoti kekuatan umat.

Dinasti Umayyah, meskipun sering dikritik oleh para ulama atas berbagai kebijakannya, diakui Ibn al-Athir sebagai kekuatan yang berhasil memperluas wilayah Islam hingga ke Spanyol di barat dan Sindh di timur. Beliau mencatat penaklukan-penaklukan besar ini dengan rinci, termasuk ekspedisi Thariq ibn Ziyad ke Andalusia, penaklukan Transoxiana oleh Qutaybah ibn Muslim, dan berbagai operasi militer di Asia Tengah dan Afrika Utara.

Kekhalifahan Abbasiyah digambarkan sebagai era keemasan peradaban Islam, terutama pada masa Harun al-Rasyid dan al-Ma'mun. Ibn al-Athir mendokumentasikan kemajuan ilmu pengetahuan, penerjemahan karya-karya Yunani, perkembangan ilmu fiqh dan teologi, serta kemakmuran ekonomi Baghdad sebagai pusat perdagangan dan kebudayaan dunia. Namun beliau juga tidak menutup mata terhadap konflik internal, pergolakan politik, dan melemahnya otoritas khalifah di hadapan para penguasa militer (amir al-umara').

Periode Abbasiyah akhir, yang ditandai dengan meningkatnya pengaruh para amir Buyid dan kemudian Saljuk, menggambarkan fragmentasi kekuasaan Islam yang membuka celah bagi datangnya ancaman dari luar. Ibn al-Athir mencermati proses ini dengan pandangan seorang sejarawan yang memahami pola-pola sejarah jangka panjang.

Bagian ini juga mencakup munculnya dinasti-dinasti regional seperti Samaniyah, Ghaznawidah, dan Fatimiyah, yang masing-masing mewarnai peta politik dan intelektual dunia Islam dengan cara yang berbeda-beda.

Ibn al-Athir's treatment of these dynasties reflects both his admiration for Islamic political achievement and his willingness to record failures, excesses, and internal conflicts without idealization. The Umayyad Period Ibn al-Athir opens the Umayyad period with the الخلافة of Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan, treating the civil wars that brought the Umayyads to power with characteristic balance. He records the events of Siffin, the arbitration at Adhruh, and the establishment of Umayyad rule in Damascus without taking sides in the theological and political disputes that divided subsequent مسلم opinion. His priority is to reconstruct what happened, not to adjudicate who was الحق. The conquests under the Umayyads receive detailed coverage. The expansion into North Africa and Andalus under al-Walid ibn Abd al-مالك, the campaigns into المحوري Asia under Qutayba ibn مسلم, and the naval expeditions into the Mediterranean are narrated with the enthusiasm of a historian who appreciates military logistics and geography. These sections demonstrate that Al-Kamil is not merely a political chronicle but an attempt لفهم the forces that shape مسلم civilization. The early Abbasid centuries under al-Mansur, Harun al-Rashid, and al-Mamun represent for Ibn al-Athir the apex of Islamic civilization: the translation movement that absorbed Greek philosophy and science, the flourishing of Baghdad as the الدنيا's greatest city, the codification of Islamic law, and the development of الحديث scholarship. The accounts of Harun al-Rashid are among the most vivid in the chronicle, drawing on court traditions about his legendary الكرم, his judicial interest, and his personal piety alongside his political pragmatism.
